payload.answer: Knowledge graphs model real-world information as interconnected data. They consist of nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (attributes). They enable advanced queries, inference, and interoperability across data sources. Common examples include semantic web graphs (RDF/OWL), property graphs, and knowledge bases like Wikidata. If you want, I can tailor an introduction to basics, data modeling patterns, or practical tutorials.

entity_resolution.reason: No explicit entities requested; provide foundational knowledge first.

memory_cards.statement: A knowledge graph is a structured representation of information where entities (things) are nodes and relationships (predicates) are edges, enabling semantic queries and reasoning.
Core components include nodes (entities), edges (relationships), and properties (attributes) of nodes/edges.
